UC Police Spending, 2011-2024: Annual and Cumulative Nominal Series

Robert J. Dellinger

March 31, 2026

  • Panel overview
    • Source basis
    • Source documentation and cohort definition
    • Spending logic and estimands
  • Setup and load packages
  • Time-window validation for the police panel
  • Yearly source files entering the police panel
  • Construction of annual and cumulative police spending series
  • Final police spending tables and figures
    • Annual and role-group tables
    • Rendered figures by estimand
    • Annual total-pay series
    • Cumulative spending series
    • Pay-component mix
  • Report-level validation checks
  • Saved tables, figures, and report artifacts
  • Closing interpretation
  • Works Cited

This report is the repository’s person-level UC police annual panel. It traces nominal annual and cumulative spending from the first available Transparent California University of California year through the latest available 2024 year and keeps the person-level police cohort separate from the title-summary estimate workflows elsewhere in the repository.

Interpretive note: the current Transparent California University of California page is 2024, but it remains labeled Data Collection in Progress (Transparent California 2026b). This report therefore treats 2024 as the latest available University of California year rather than a finalized census, uses person-level annual sums rather than pooled multi-year averages, and treats total_pay as the primary long-run series because reported benefits are zero in the earliest years.

Panel overview

The objective is to build a publication-quality, reusable University of California police-spending series that shows how annual and cumulative police salary spending changed from the first available Transparent California UC year through the latest available UC salary year in the public Transparent California database.

Source basis

The workflow uses Transparent California’s University of California salary search database (Transparent California 2026a). As of March 31, 2026, the current UC agency page is 2024 and that page is explicitly labeled “2024 Data Collection in Progress” (Transparent California 2026b). This script intentionally uses the latest available 2024 UC records, including the in-progress 2024 page, so the series runs through 2024 rather than stopping at 2023. All core spending results in this report are person-level annual sums from the Transparent California police cohort after the title-based false-positive filter is applied.

Source documentation and cohort definition

Transparent California exposes record-level pay components for each employee, including regular_pay, overtime_pay, other_pay, total_pay, benefits, and total_pay_benefits. Transparent California’s FAQ states that these values are annual amounts for the reported year, that some agencies report on a fiscal-year basis and some on a calendar-year basis, that benefit values reflect employer-paid benefit costs rather than take-home cash, and that the data are posted as supplied by reporting agencies. The public search can match names as well as job titles, so the workflow first scrapes the full yearly POLICE candidate pool and then keeps only rows whose cleaned job title literally contains POLICE. That title filter is used to remove false positives while preserving the police-titled workforce that organizers are usually trying to measure. Because the newest UC page is still marked in progress, the final year in this panel should be treated as the latest available snapshot rather than a frozen final census. In the current UC police series, the 2011 and 2012 rows report zero benefits in aggregate, so the primary long-run series uses total_pay as the most consistent salary-spending measure and treats total_pay_benefits as a secondary employer-compensation measure.

Spending logic and estimands

The workflow builds a year-by-year police dataset and then summarizes both annual and cumulative spending. This is an annual panel design, not a pooled multi-year average. The resulting totals are nominal dollars, not inflation-adjusted dollars, so the time series should be interpreted as administrative spending growth rather than real purchasing-power growth. The key measures are:

  • annual_total_pay_y = Σ total_pay_i
  • annual_total_pay_benefits_y = Σ total_pay_benefits_i
  • cumulative_total_pay_t = Σ annual_total_pay_y for all years from the first available year through year t
  • cumulative_total_pay_benefits_t = Σ annual_total_pay_benefits_y for all years from the first available year through year t
  • year_over_year_growth_t = (annual_total_pay_benefits_t / annual_total_pay_benefits_(t-1)) - 1

The report also tracks the overtime share of police payroll because overtime is often a central organizing and budgeting question:

  • overtime_share_of_total_pay_y = annual_overtime_pay_y / annual_total_pay_y

Setup and load packages

This section loads helper functions, packages, and project paths.

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)

utility_path <- if (file.exists("scripts/functions/uc_salary_utils.R")) {
  "scripts/functions/uc_salary_utils.R"
} else {
  "functions/uc_salary_utils.R"
}

source(utility_path)
uc_salary_load_packages()

police_search_term <- "POLICE"
analysis_start_year <- 2011L
year_status <- uc_salary_get_agency_year_status(agency_slug = "university-of-california")
analysis_end_year <- year_status$current_page_year[[1]]
analysis_years <- seq.int(analysis_start_year, analysis_end_year)
series_stub <- paste0("uc_police_spending_", analysis_start_year, "_", analysis_end_year)

detail_output <- uc_salary_path("data", "processed", paste0(series_stub, "_detail.csv"))
annual_output <- uc_salary_path("outputs", "tables", paste0(series_stub, "_annual_summary.csv"))
component_output <- uc_salary_path("outputs", "tables", paste0(series_stub, "_component_summary.csv"))
role_output <- uc_salary_path("outputs", "tables", paste0(series_stub, "_role_summary.csv"))
scrape_manifest_output <- uc_salary_path("outputs", "logs", "qa", paste0(series_stub, "_scrape_manifest.csv"))
qa_output <- uc_salary_path("outputs", "logs", "qa", paste0(series_stub, "_qa_summary.csv"))
figure_manifest_output <- uc_salary_path("outputs", "logs", "qa", paste0(series_stub, "_figure_manifest.csv"))

uc_salary_ensure_directory(dirname(detail_output))
uc_salary_ensure_directory(dirname(annual_output))
uc_salary_ensure_directory(dirname(scrape_manifest_output))

Time-window validation for the police panel

These checks confirm that the latest-available analysis window is internally consistent before the script begins scraping or reusing yearly police extracts.

stopifnot(!is.na(analysis_end_year))
stopifnot(analysis_end_year >= analysis_start_year)
stopifnot(length(analysis_years) > 0)

validation_summary <- tibble::tibble(
  validation_check = c(
    "Current Transparent California UC page year",
    "Current page marked as in progress",
    "Latest available year used in this script",
    "First year used in this script",
    "Number of years in the police-spending series"
  ),
  validation_result = c(
    year_status$current_page_year[[1]],
    year_status$data_collection_in_progress[[1]],
    analysis_end_year,
    analysis_start_year,
    length(analysis_years)
  )
)

knitr::kable(validation_summary)
validation_check validation_result
Current Transparent California UC page year 2024
Current page marked as in progress 1
Latest available year used in this script 2024
First year used in this script 2011
Number of years in the police-spending series 14

Yearly source files entering the police panel

This section identifies which yearly police raw extracts already exist locally and which years will be scraped from Transparent California on this run. The manifest below is an execution-planning artifact rather than an empirical result.

planned_input_manifest <- tibble::tibble(
  year = analysis_years,
  raw_file = file.path(
    uc_salary_path("data", "raw", "transparent_california"),
    as.character(analysis_years),
    paste0("tc_", analysis_years, "_police.csv")
  )
) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(raw_file_exists = file.exists(raw_file))

planned_input_manifest %>%
  dplyr::mutate(raw_file = uc_salary_relpath(raw_file)) %>%
  knitr::kable()
year raw_file raw_file_exists
2011 data/raw/transparent_california/2011/tc_2011_police.csv TRUE
2012 data/raw/transparent_california/2012/tc_2012_police.csv TRUE
2013 data/raw/transparent_california/2013/tc_2013_police.csv TRUE
2014 data/raw/transparent_california/2014/tc_2014_police.csv TRUE
2015 data/raw/transparent_california/2015/tc_2015_police.csv TRUE
2016 data/raw/transparent_california/2016/tc_2016_police.csv TRUE
2017 data/raw/transparent_california/2017/tc_2017_police.csv TRUE
2018 data/raw/transparent_california/2018/tc_2018_police.csv TRUE
2019 data/raw/transparent_california/2019/tc_2019_police.csv TRUE
2020 data/raw/transparent_california/2020/tc_2020_police.csv TRUE
2021 data/raw/transparent_california/2021/tc_2021_police.csv TRUE
2022 data/raw/transparent_california/2022/tc_2022_police.csv TRUE
2023 data/raw/transparent_california/2023/tc_2023_police.csv TRUE
2024 data/raw/transparent_california/2024/tc_2024_police.csv TRUE

Construction of annual and cumulative police spending series

This section scrapes or reuses yearly police extracts, filters the analytic police cohort, summarizes annual and cumulative spending, and writes publication-ready graphics and tables. Analytically, it proceeds in four steps: building the yearly police candidate pool, applying the literal POLICE title filter, summarizing annual and cumulative nominal spending, and saving the tables and figures that represent the panel.

yearly_results <- purrr::map(
  analysis_years,
  function(year_value) {
    raw_year_directory <- uc_salary_path("data", "raw", "transparent_california", as.character(year_value))
    raw_output_path <- file.path(raw_year_directory, paste0("tc_", year_value, "_police.csv"))

    uc_salary_ensure_directory(raw_year_directory)

    # Reuse cached yearly police files when they already exist to keep reruns fast and stable.
    year_candidate_pool <- if (file.exists(raw_output_path)) {
      cached_year_extract <- readr::read_csv(raw_output_path, show_col_types = FALSE)

      if (uc_salary_cache_is_current(cached_year_extract)) {
        cached_year_extract
      } else {
        raw_year_extract <- uc_salary_scrape_query(
          search_term = police_search_term,
          year = year_value,
          pause_seconds = 0.20,
          verbose = TRUE
        )

        cleaned_year_extract <- uc_salary_clean_raw_extract(raw_year_extract, query_slug = "police")
        readr::write_csv(cleaned_year_extract, raw_output_path)
        cleaned_year_extract
      }
    } else {
      raw_year_extract <- uc_salary_scrape_query(
        search_term = police_search_term,
        year = year_value,
        pause_seconds = 0.20,
        verbose = TRUE
      )

      cleaned_year_extract <- uc_salary_clean_raw_extract(raw_year_extract, query_slug = "police")
      readr::write_csv(cleaned_year_extract, raw_output_path)
      cleaned_year_extract
    }

    # Keep only job titles that truly belong to the police cohort rather than name-match false positives.
    analytic_year_data <- year_candidate_pool %>%
      dplyr::filter(stringr::str_detect(job_title_clean, "\\bPOLICE\\b")) %>%
      dplyr::mutate(
        police_role_group = dplyr::case_when(
          stringr::str_detect(job_title_clean, "CHF|CAPTAIN|LIEUTENANT|SERGEANT SUPV|SERGEANT") ~ "Police leadership and supervision",
          TRUE ~ "Police officers and other police titles"
        )
      )

    manifest_row <- tibble::tibble(
      year = year_value,
      source_url = dplyr::first(year_candidate_pool$source_url),
      expected_total_records = max(year_candidate_pool$source_total_records, na.rm = TRUE),
      candidate_rows_scraped = nrow(year_candidate_pool),
      analytic_police_rows = nrow(analytic_year_data),
      non_police_rows_removed = nrow(year_candidate_pool) - nrow(analytic_year_data),
      total_pages_reported = max(year_candidate_pool$source_total_pages, na.rm = TRUE),
      unique_pages_scraped = dplyr::n_distinct(year_candidate_pool$source_page),
      raw_file = raw_output_path
    )

    list(data = analytic_year_data, manifest = manifest_row)
  }
)

police_detail <- dplyr::bind_rows(purrr::map(yearly_results, "data"))
scrape_manifest <- dplyr::bind_rows(purrr::map(yearly_results, "manifest"))

annual_spending <- police_detail %>%
  dplyr::group_by(year) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(
    workers = dplyr::n(),
    unique_job_titles = dplyr::n_distinct(job_title_clean),
    total_regular_pay = sum(regular_pay, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_overtime_pay = sum(overtime_pay, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_other_pay = sum(other_pay, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_pay = sum(total_pay, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_benefits = sum(benefits, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_pay_benefits = sum(total_pay_benefits, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_total_pay_benefits = stats::median(total_pay_benefits, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_total_pay_benefits = mean(total_pay_benefits, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(year) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    overtime_share_of_total_pay = dplyr::if_else(total_pay > 0, total_overtime_pay / total_pay, NA_real_),
    benefits_share_of_total_comp = dplyr::if_else(total_pay_benefits > 0, total_benefits / total_pay_benefits, NA_real_),
    annual_change_total_pay_benefits = (total_pay_benefits / dplyr::lag(total_pay_benefits)) - 1,
    annual_change_total_pay = (total_pay / dplyr::lag(total_pay)) - 1,
    cumulative_total_pay = cumsum(total_pay),
    cumulative_total_pay_benefits = cumsum(total_pay_benefits),
    total_pay_benefits_index_start_year = 100 * total_pay_benefits / dplyr::first(total_pay_benefits),
    workers_index_start_year = 100 * workers / dplyr::first(workers)
  )

component_spending <- annual_spending %>%
  dplyr::select(year, total_regular_pay, total_overtime_pay, total_other_pay, total_benefits) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = c(total_regular_pay, total_overtime_pay, total_other_pay, total_benefits),
    names_to = "pay_component",
    values_to = "annual_amount"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    pay_component = factor(
      pay_component,
      levels = c("total_regular_pay", "total_overtime_pay", "total_other_pay", "total_benefits"),
      labels = c("Regular pay", "Overtime pay", "Other pay", "Benefits")
    )
  )

role_spending <- police_detail %>%
  dplyr::group_by(year, police_role_group) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(
    workers = dplyr::n(),
    total_pay = sum(total_pay, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_pay_benefits = sum(total_pay_benefits, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_total_pay_benefits = stats::median(total_pay_benefits, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

years_with_zero_benefits <- annual_spending %>%
  dplyr::filter(total_benefits == 0) %>%
  dplyr::pull(year)

starting_total_pay <- dplyr::first(annual_spending$total_pay)
ending_total_pay <- dplyr::last(annual_spending$total_pay)
ending_cumulative_pay <- dplyr::last(annual_spending$cumulative_total_pay)
absolute_increase <- ending_total_pay - starting_total_pay
percent_increase <- (ending_total_pay / starting_total_pay) - 1
dollar_formatter <- scales::label_dollar(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())

plot_annual_salary_spending <- annual_spending %>%
  ggplot2::ggplot(ggplot2::aes(x = year, y = total_pay)) +
  ggplot2::geom_col(fill = uc_salary_palette[["Police"]], width = 0.72) +
  ggplot2::geom_text(
    ggplot2::aes(label = scales::label_dollar(scale = 1e-6, suffix = "M", accuracy = 0.1)(total_pay)),
    vjust = -0.35,
    size = 3.0,
    colour = "#1F2933"
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(breaks = analysis_years) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::label_dollar(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale()),
    expand = ggplot2::expansion(mult = c(0, 0.14))
  ) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = uc_salary_wrap_text(paste0("Annual UC police total pay, ", analysis_start_year, "-", analysis_end_year), 62),
    subtitle = uc_salary_wrap_text(paste0(
      "Person-level nominal annual sums from Transparent California for UC job titles containing POLICE. Total pay rose by ",
      dollar_formatter(absolute_increase),
      " (",
      scales::label_percent(accuracy = 0.1)(percent_increase),
      ") from ",
      analysis_start_year,
      " to ",
      analysis_end_year,
      "."
    ), 105),
    x = NULL,
    y = "Annual total pay",
    caption = uc_salary_wrap_text(paste0(
      "Source type: person-level Transparent California realized pay in nominal dollars. The primary series uses total pay because reported benefits are zero in ",
      paste(years_with_zero_benefits, collapse = " and "),
      ". ",
      analysis_end_year,
      " is the latest available UC year and remains in progress."
    ), 135)
  ) +
  uc_salary_theme()

cumulative_spending_long <- annual_spending %>%
  dplyr::select(year, cumulative_total_pay, cumulative_total_pay_benefits) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = c(cumulative_total_pay, cumulative_total_pay_benefits),
    names_to = "measure_type",
    values_to = "cumulative_amount"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    measure_type = factor(
      measure_type,
      levels = c("cumulative_total_pay", "cumulative_total_pay_benefits"),
      labels = c("Cumulative total pay", "Cumulative total pay and benefits")
    )
  )

plot_cumulative_spending <- cumulative_spending_long %>%
  ggplot2::ggplot(ggplot2::aes(x = year, y = cumulative_amount, colour = measure_type)) +
  ggplot2::geom_line(linewidth = 1.4) +
  ggplot2::geom_point(size = 2.8) +
  ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(breaks = analysis_years) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::label_dollar(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale()),
    expand = ggplot2::expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.12))
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(
      "Cumulative total pay" = "#8D99AE",
      "Cumulative total pay and benefits" = "#8B1E3F"
    )
  ) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = uc_salary_wrap_text(paste0("Cumulative UC police spending, ", analysis_start_year, "-", analysis_end_year), 62),
    subtitle = uc_salary_wrap_text(paste0(
      "Cumulative nominal total pay reached ",
      dollar_formatter(ending_cumulative_pay),
      " by ",
      analysis_end_year,
      "; the second line adds reported employer-paid benefits where Transparent California records them."
    ), 105),
    x = NULL,
    y = "Cumulative dollars",
    colour = NULL,
    caption = uc_salary_wrap_text(paste0(
      "Source type: person-level Transparent California annual sums accumulated over time in nominal dollars. Benefits are zero in ",
      paste(years_with_zero_benefits, collapse = " and "),
      ". The ",
      analysis_end_year,
      " endpoint is the latest available UC year and may change while 2024 collection remains in progress."
    ), 135)
  ) +
  uc_salary_theme()

plot_component_mix <- component_spending %>%
  ggplot2::ggplot(ggplot2::aes(x = factor(year), y = annual_amount, fill = pay_component)) +
  ggplot2::geom_col(width = 0.74) +
  ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Regular pay" = "#3E7CB1",
      "Overtime pay" = "#D1495B",
      "Other pay" = "#EDA247",
      "Benefits" = "#4D9078"
    )
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::label_dollar(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale()),
    expand = ggplot2::expansion(mult = c(0, 0.07))
  ) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = uc_salary_wrap_text(paste0("Annual UC police pay components, ", analysis_start_year, "-", analysis_end_year), 62),
    subtitle = uc_salary_wrap_text(paste0(
      "Stacked nominal annual sums show regular pay, overtime pay, other pay, and reported employer-paid benefits for UC job titles containing POLICE from ",
      analysis_start_year,
      " through ",
      analysis_end_year,
      "."
    ), 105),
    x = NULL,
    y = "Annual dollars",
    fill = NULL,
    caption = uc_salary_wrap_text(paste0(
      "Source type: person-level Transparent California annual sums in nominal dollars for UC job titles containing POLICE. Reported benefits are employer-paid costs and are zero in ",
      paste(years_with_zero_benefits, collapse = " and "),
      "."
    ), 135)
  ) +
  uc_salary_theme()

figure_manifest <- tibble::tibble(
  figure = c(
    "Annual police salary spending",
    "Cumulative police spending",
    "Annual police pay-component mix"
  ),
  output_path = c(
    uc_salary_save_plot(plot_annual_salary_spending, paste0(series_stub, "_annual_salary_spending.png"), width = 13, height = 8.5),
    uc_salary_save_plot(plot_cumulative_spending, paste0(series_stub, "_cumulative_salary_spending.png"), width = 13, height = 8.5),
    uc_salary_save_plot(plot_component_mix, paste0(series_stub, "_annual_component_mix.png"), width = 12, height = 7.5)
  )
)

readr::write_csv(police_detail, detail_output)
readr::write_csv(annual_spending, annual_output)
readr::write_csv(component_spending, component_output)
readr::write_csv(role_spending, role_output)
readr::write_csv(scrape_manifest, scrape_manifest_output)
readr::write_csv(figure_manifest, figure_manifest_output)

Final police spending tables and figures

This section reviews the annual totals and role-group summaries before the final QA checks, then renders the saved figures inline so the HTML report shows the empirical police series directly.

Annual and role-group tables

These tables are the tabular backbone for the figures that follow: annual totals establish the nominal panel, and the role-group table shows how the police cohort is internally structured across years.

annual_spending %>%
  dplyr::select(
    year,
    workers,
    total_pay,
    total_pay_benefits,
    annual_change_total_pay,
    cumulative_total_pay,
    overtime_share_of_total_pay
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable()
year workers total_pay total_pay_benefits annual_change_total_pay cumulative_total_pay overtime_share_of_total_pay

2023 451 68170959 75099776 0.1385637 636254194 0.1433565
2024 462 79938707 87730428 0.1726211 716192901 0.1734271 
role_spending %>%
  dplyr::arrange(year, police_role_group) %>%
  dplyr::select(year, police_role_group, workers, total_pay, total_pay_benefits, median_total_pay_benefits) %>%
  knitr::kable()

Rendered figures by estimand

The figures below are the same outputs written to outputs/figures/, rendered inline so the HTML report can be read as the main public-facing product.

Annual total-pay series

This figure is the primary long-run police-spending series because it rests on the most consistent reported cash-pay field across all years in the panel.

plot_annual_salary_spending

Cumulative spending series

This figure answers a different question than annual pay levels: it accumulates nominal spending over the full 2011-2024 period.

plot_cumulative_spending

Pay-component mix

This figure shows how regular pay, overtime, other pay, and reported benefits combine inside the annual police payroll bill.

plot_component_mix

Report-level validation checks

This section checks scrape completeness, annual coverage, and whether the expected output files were written successfully. Together these checks confirm that the annual panel is complete through the latest available year used in the report.

qa_summary <- tibble::tibble(
  qa_metric = c(
    "Years analyzed",
    "Years with matching expected and observed candidate rows",
    "Total police analytic rows across all years",
    "Years represented in annual summary",
    "Rows removed by the POLICE title filter",
    "Years with zero reported benefits totals",
    "Figures written",
    "Latest available year used"
  ),
  qa_value = c(
    length(analysis_years),
    sum(scrape_manifest$expected_total_records == scrape_manifest$candidate_rows_scraped, na.rm = TRUE),
    nrow(police_detail),
    nrow(annual_spending),
    sum(scrape_manifest$non_police_rows_removed, na.rm = TRUE),
    length(years_with_zero_benefits),
    nrow(figure_manifest),
    analysis_end_year
  )
)

readr::write_csv(qa_summary, qa_output)

qa_summary %>%
  knitr::kable()
qa_metric qa_value
Years analyzed 14
Years with matching expected and observed candidate rows 14
Total police analytic rows across all years 6058
Years represented in annual summary 14
Rows removed by the POLICE title filter 6
Years with zero reported benefits totals 2
Figures written 3
Latest available year used 2024

Saved tables, figures, and report artifacts

This artifact registry functions as a reproducibility appendix rather than a findings table. The labels distinguish the empirical police detail file from annual summary tables, execution logs, and exported figures.

export_manifest <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  tibble::tibble(
    artifact = c(
      "Police detail dataset (person-level annual panel)",
      "Annual police spending summary",
      "Annual police component summary",
      "Annual police role-group summary",
      "Police scrape manifest",
      "Police series QA summary",
      "Police figure manifest"
    ),
    output_path = c(
      detail_output,
      annual_output,
      component_output,
      role_output,
      scrape_manifest_output,
      qa_output,
      figure_manifest_output
    )
  ),
  figure_manifest %>%
    dplyr::rename(artifact = figure)
)

export_manifest %>%
  dplyr::mutate(output_path = uc_salary_relpath(output_path)) %>%
  knitr::kable()
artifact output_path
Police detail dataset (person-level annual panel) data/processed/uc_police_spending_2011_2024_detail.csv
Annual police spending summary outputs/tables/uc_police_spending_2011_2024_annual_summary.csv
Annual police component summary outputs/tables/uc_police_spending_2011_2024_component_summary.csv
Annual police role-group summary outputs/tables/uc_police_spending_2011_2024_role_summary.csv
Police scrape manifest outputs/logs/qa/uc_police_spending_2011_2024_scrape_manifest.csv
Police series QA summary outputs/logs/qa/uc_police_spending_2011_2024_qa_summary.csv
Police figure manifest outputs/logs/qa/uc_police_spending_2011_2024_figure_manifest.csv
Annual police salary spending outputs/figures/uc_police_spending_2011_2024_annual_salary_spending.png
Cumulative police spending outputs/figures/uc_police_spending_2011_2024_cumulative_salary_spending.png
Annual police pay-component mix outputs/figures/uc_police_spending_2011_2024_annual_component_mix.png

Closing interpretation

This report shows a nominal annual panel of observed University of California police spending rather than an inflation-adjusted real-dollar trend or an average salary series. Its strongest headline results are the annual total_pay series and the cumulative spending totals, which answer related but different questions and should not be read as interchangeable measures.

Works Cited

This section is reserved for the bibliography generated from the report citations.

Transparent California. 2026a. 2011–2024 Salaries for University of California. https://transparentcalifornia.com/salaries/university-of-california/.
Transparent California. 2026b. 2024 Salaries for University of California. https://transparentcalifornia.com/salaries/2024/university-of-california/.
